answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: Orderly (1.5°C) — represented by NGFS RM NZ Net Zero
Too Little Too Late (>2°C) — represented by NGFS RM NDC (Nationally Determined Contributions)
Hothouse (>3°C) — represented by NGFS RM CP (Current Policies)
answers.evidence: "Too Little, Too Late | Orderly | Hothouse | IEA STEPS. IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 NGFS NDCs* NGFS Net Zero 2050 ... Figure 4: Scenario datasets utilised to assess in various scenarios" and "Scenario 1: Orderly (1.5°C) ... Chosen scenario to represent the Orderly scenario: NGFS RM NZ — Net Zero*" and "Chosen scenario to represent the Too Little Too Late Scenario: NGFS RM NDC — Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)*" and "Chosen scenario to represent the Hothouse scenario: NGFS RM CP — Current Policies"
answers.notes: Three FSC narrative scenarios (Orderly, Too Little Too Late, Hothouse) are the primary scenarios used for risk assessment, each mapped to a specific NGFS scenario. IEA STEPS and IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 and NGFS Net Zero 2050 are also listed as scenario datasets in Figure 4.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Orderly scenario), >2°C / 2.6°C (Too Little Too Late scenario), and >3°C / ~3°C (Hothouse scenario). Temperature overshoot is also mentioned: NGFS Net Zero 2050 has "no or low overshoot (<0.1°C) of 1.5°C in earlier years."
answers.evidence: "NGFS RM NZ — Net Zero* ... giving at least a 50% chance of limiting global warming to below 1.5°C by the end of the century, with no or low overshoot (<0.1°C) of 1.5°C in earlier years." | "Emissions decline but lead nonetheless to 2.6°C of warming associated with moderate to severe physical risks." | "Emissions grow until 2080 leading to about 3°C of warming and severe physical risks."
answers.notes: Overshoot is explicitly defined in the glossary: "Overshoot is the term used by the IPCC to describe scenarios in which a specified global warming temperature level is exceeded — typically between 1.5 and 2°C — before returning to that level at some point in the future."

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), IEA (International Energy Agency), and FSC (Financial Services Council of New Zealand). ISS ESG is named as the data/analytics provider that uses NGFS and IEA scenario data.
answers.evidence: "The climate impact assessment within the ISS ESG solution contains climate scenario analysis and modelling using Network for Greening Financial Sector (NGFS) scenario data and the International Energy Agency (IEA)." and "Fisher Funds is using the FSC New Zealand Climate Scenario Narratives for the Financial Services Sector as a guide to help in developing consistent and comparable information."
answers.notes: NGFS and IEA provide the underlying scenario data; FSC provides the narrative framework; ISS ESG is the analytics platform that operationalises the scenarios.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: The document references NGFS and IEA scenarios exclusively. No SSP or RCP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the document.
answers.notes: The document uses NGFS scenario families (Net Zero 2050, NDCs, Current Policies) and IEA scenarios (STEPS, Net Zero Emissions by 2050). No SSP or RCP labels appear.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "These narratives have been adjusted to reflect the investments of Marlin for use in conjunction with the NGFS scenarios." and "The IMT confirmed the time horizons to be used, reviewed the FSC climate scenario narratives, and participated in climate scenario narrative workshops which also supported their analysis of Marlin. As a result, the IMT determined that 2025 is too short as a time horizon and Fisher Funds believes that more of these societal shifts (as referred to in the FSC document) may be seen by 2030. Altering the short-term time horizon also meant that the medium-term time horizon was set to 2040."
answers.notes: The FSC narratives were explicitly "adjusted to reflect the investments of Marlin." Time horizons were also modified from the FSC defaults (2025 short-term changed to 2030). This constitutes adaptation for local/entity-specific context.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "The IMT confirmed the time horizons to be used, reviewed the FSC climate scenario narratives, and participated in climate scenario narrative workshops which also supported their analysis of Marlin."
answers.notes: Internal workshops were held (IMT participated in "climate scenario narrative workshops"), but these appear to be internal review/adaptation workshops rather than participatory or co-produced scenario development with external stakeholders. No language such as "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", or "collaborative development" with external parties is used.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described against which the climate scenarios are compared. The document uses three scenarios (Orderly, Too Little Too Late, Hothouse) as plausible futures without designating one as a baseline.
answers.evidence: "Fisher Funds worked to select 3 plausible versions of the future and used the scenarios for climate risk and opportunity analysis."
answers.notes: The document defines "Base year" in the glossary as the first financial year for metrics comparison (2024), but this is a metrics baseline, not a climate scenario baseline. No scenario is explicitly labelled as a reference or baseline scenario.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: "NGFS RM* NGFS REMIND-MagPIE Model which is recommended by NGFS for policy and decision-makers by focusing more on the economy and technology modeling."
answers.notes: The ISS ESG platform is used as the analytics solution but is not itself a climate model. REMIND-MAgPIE is the only named IAM/climate model. The ISS ESG physical risk assessment methodology is also referenced but not named as a specific model.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "Operational impacts were quantified by considering the costs of repairing assets damaged by tropical cyclones, river floods, coastal floods and wildfires, and the loss of income due to the associated organisational interruptions." and "The following table sets out the significant physical risks identified through the assessment process. The table provides a matrix of risks and impacts—the physical climate risk (e.g. flood, wildfire), the risk impact (e.g. operational, financial, reputational)..."
answers.notes: The document explicitly quantifies damage/loss from acute physical risks including tropical cyclones, river floods, coastal floods, and wildfires. Portfolio Value at Risk (VaR) of approximately 0.2% of AUM is also reported.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "Large increases in carbon price will drive a rapid improvement in low emissions technology efficacy and uptake." | "This includes irreversible changes like higher sea level rise." | "Operational impacts were quantified by considering the costs of repairing assets damaged by tropical cyclones, river floods, coastal floods and wildfires... The analysis also considered the impact of heat stress on labour productivity." | "Market impacts were quantified by estimating the revenue at risk due to nationwide effects on country gross domestic products (GDPs) due to the combined impact of droughts and heat stress on agricultural productivity, decrease in labour productivity, and human health effects."
answers.notes: These variables appear across the scenario narratives and the physical risk assessment methodology sections.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "Scenario analysis takes inputs of entity carbon emissions, and global climate scenario parameters to assess the potential financial outcomes for entities..." and the physical risk heat map shows all risks assessed at "Global" region level. The NGFS and IEA scenarios used are global in scope with no mention of downscaling to regional or local level.
answers.notes: The physical risk heat map (Figure 5, page 15) lists "Global" as the region for all assessed risks. No regional or local downscaling is described.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: to 2030; Medium-term: to 2040; Long-term: to 2050. Rationales are provided for each timeframe.
answers.evidence: "The IMT confirmed the time horizons to be used... the IMT determined that 2025 is too short as a time horizon and Fisher Funds believes that more of these societal shifts (as referred to in the FSC document) may be seen by 2030. Altering the short-term time horizon also meant that the medium-term time horizon was set to 2040. The long-term horizon was retained at 2050." | Short-term rationale: "Aligns with many interim targets of companies invested in. Captures the impact of climate change for investors who may have liquidation events in this timeframe." | Medium-term rationale: "More likely to capture the impact of policy changes in countries around the world as they aim to set up frameworks to encourage decarbonisation." | Long-term rationale: "Captures the impact of climate change over a long time horizon where impacts are more likely to be present in the economy."
answers.notes: Rationales are explicitly provided for each time horizon. The short-term was deliberately changed from the FSC default of 2025 to 2030 with justification given.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "Fisher Funds recognises that for investors to make informed decisions, it is useful for information on investment products from all providers to be comparable. The Financial Services Council (FSC) of New Zealand also recognises this and has created climate scenario narratives that can be adopted by the industry. Fisher Funds is using the FSC New Zealand Climate Scenario Narratives for the Financial Services Sector as a guide to help in developing consistent and comparable information." and "Fisher Funds is satisfied that the FSC narratives have been suitably stated for use by financial services CREs. These narratives are underpinned by robust analysis and are therefore suitable (as adjusted for Fisher Funds) to use in this climate statement. Use of FSC narratives supports comparable and consistent disclosures in the industry which Fisher Funds also wishes to support to the extent appropriate."
answers.notes: The justification centres on: (1) industry comparability and consistency via FSC narratives; (2) the FSC narratives being "underpinned by robust analysis"; (3) the NGFS REMIND-MAgPIE model being "recommended by NGFS for policy and decision-makers by focusing more on the economy and technology modeling" (page 34). The choice of ISS ESG as the analytics platform is also implicitly justified by its role as "a global provider of environmental, social and governance data and analysis."
